era_context: The early 14th century saw the fragmentation of Mongol unity, the consolidation of European feudalism, and the rise of maritime trade networks. The Yuan Dynasty under Ayurbarwada ruled China; the Mamluk Sultanate dominated the Eastern Mediterranean; the Papacy remained in Avignon; and the Scottish Wars of Independence were reaching a critical phase. This was an era of climate instability (the onset of the Little Ice Age), famine cycles, and religious reform movements.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically recorded)
Southeast Asian kingdoms (sparse European documentation; local chronicles less accessible to Western scholarship)
Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records for this period)

events.title: Battle of Bannockburn
events.description: Robert I of Scotland defeated Edward II of England at Bannockburn near Stirling, securing Scottish independence de facto and forcing English recognition of Scottish sovereignty. The victory consolidated Robert Bruce's position as King of Scots and ended the immediate threat of English conquest. The battle became a foundational moment in Scottish national identity and demonstrated the vulnerability of English feudal cavalry to disciplined infantry formations.
key_figures: Robert I (Robert the Bruce), King of Scotland

sources.name: Barbour, John. The Brus (compiled early 15th century, based on oral tradition and earlier chronicles)
Scalacronica by Sir Thomas Gray (14th-century account)

events.certainty_note: The battle is well-attested in contemporary and near-contemporary chronicles. The date (23–24 June 1314) is secure. Tactical details vary between sources but the outcome is uncontested.

events.title: Great Famine begins in Northern Europe
events.region: Northern Europe (England, Scotland, Ireland, Scandinavia, Low Countries, Northern France, Germany)
events.description: Severe crop failures triggered by prolonged wet weather and cold temperatures began across Northern Europe, initiating a subsistence crisis that would persist through 1317. Grain prices soared; livestock died; malnutrition weakened populations and increased susceptibility to disease. The famine killed an estimated 5–10% of the population in affected regions and destabilized political structures. This climatic shock is attributed to volcanic aerosols and the onset of the Little Ice Age.

sources.name: Lanercost Chronicle (contemporary monastic record from northern England)
Historiographic consensus from climate and demographic studies (e.g., Campbell, Benedictow)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary chronicles document the famine's onset in 1314–1315. Modern paleoclimatic and dendrochronological evidence confirms the climatic trigger. The scale and duration are reconstructed from grain price data and mortality records.

events.title: Clement V dies; Papacy enters sede vacante
events.description: Pope Clement V died on 20 April 1314, ending a papacy marked by the relocation of the papal seat from Rome to Avignon (1309) and the suppression of the Knights Templar. His death left the Papacy vacant for over two years, during which the College of Cardinals remained deadlocked over the succession. This interregnum reflected deep divisions within the Church over reform, temporal power, and relations with secular rulers.

sources.name: Villani, Giovanni. Nuova Cronica (Florentine chronicle, 14th century)

events.certainty_note: The date of Clement V's death (20 April 1314) is well-documented in papal records. The sede vacante lasted until the election of John XXII in August 1316.
cross_references: Follows from 1309-001 (Clement V moves papacy to Avignon)

events.title: Louis X of France dies; succession crisis begins
events.description: King Louis X of France died on 5 June 1314, leaving only a pregnant widow, Clementia of Hungary. His death triggered a succession dispute: his brother Philip V claimed the throne, arguing that a woman could not inherit the French crown (establishing the Salic Law precedent). The unborn child, John I, was born posthumously in November 1314 but died within days. Philip V's succession was contested but ultimately accepted, setting a precedent that would shape French succession law for centuries.
key_figures: Philip V, Count of Poitou (later King)
events.certainty_note: Louis X's death date (5 June 1314) and the succession dispute are well-documented. John I's birth and death in 1314 are confirmed by multiple sources.
cross_references: Led to 1316-002 (Philip V's consolidation of power)
Part of broader succession disputes in 1320s–1330s

events.title: Ayurbarwada assumes full power in Yuan Dynasty
events.description: Ayurbarwada (Emperor Renzong), who had been co-regent, consolidated full imperial authority after the death of his regent Timur Khan. His reign marked a shift toward Confucian governance and the restoration of the civil service examination system, which had been suspended. This represented a partial sinicization of Yuan rule and an attempt to stabilize the dynasty after decades of factional conflict among Mongol princes.

sources.name: Yuan Shi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty, compiled 14th century)

events.certainty_note: Ayurbarwada's consolidation of power is attested in Yuan records, though the exact chronology of his assumption of full authority is somewhat unclear. The restoration of the examination system is confirmed but may have occurred over 1313–1314.
cross_references: Part of broader Yuan political stabilization (1310–1320)
Led to 1320-001 (Ayurbarwada's death and succession crisis)

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of Bannockburn has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship: while it was a major victory, it did not immediately secure Scottish independence (which required the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton in 1328). Some historians argue that the battle's symbolic importance has been inflated in Scottish historiography relative to its immediate political impact. The Great Famine's onset date is sometimes placed in 1315 rather than 1314, depending on regional sources; the wet weather began in 1314 but widespread crop failure and famine conditions intensified in 1315. Louis X's death and the succession dispute are well-attested, but the 'Salic Law' was not formally codified until later; the exclusion of women was a pragmatic decision that was later rationalized as ancient law.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1314 is strong for Western Europe (England, France, Papacy) due to abundant monastic chronicles and royal records. Chinese sources (Yuan Shi) are detailed but compiled retrospectively and reflect court perspectives. Scottish sources are limited to English chronicles and later Scottish compilations; Scottish perspectives are underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Indian subcontinent are poorly represented in surviving written records accessible to Western scholarship. The Great Famine is well-documented in Northern European chronicles but less so in Southern Europe and the Mediterranean, suggesting regional variation that is not fully captured. Bias toward political and military events over economic and social history is inherent in the surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Bannockburn secured Scottish independence de facto; the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton (1328) formalized it de jure.
The Great Famine that began in 1314 intensified and spread in 1315–1317.
Clement V's death triggered a sede vacante that ended with John XXII's election in 1316.
Louis X's death and the succession dispute led to Philip V's consolidation of power.
Ayurbarwada's consolidation of power set the stage for the succession crisis upon his death in 1320.
